rubric: Conduct an interview — the engine that ASSESSES (the primary evidence for candidates with no public portfolio — depth is shown by explaining, not linking), VERIFIES (separates a builder from a confident résumé), and UPSKILLS (shows the candidate the bar). Two types. type='competency': probe the load-bearing claims and each competency dimension in general — ask them to explain a hard decision, what broke and how they fixed it, why X over Y, to trace a path through their own system, or to reason a problem from scratch. type='role_fit' (needs job_id): ask the questions THIS posting would ask, at its bar, using the job's graded required/preferred skills + seniority. For EACH question record: the candidate's answer (summary), a grounded EXEMPLAR ('what a great candidate at the target level / for this role would have said' — anchored to the role's requirements + the dimension rubric + the band, never a vague model opinion), a score (weak|adequate|strong), and the delta (the specific gap → an upskilling target). For owned-work claims also record ownership (sole_author|contributor|user|observer) and understanding (deep|working|shallow|cannot_explain). Be adversarial-but-supportive: tell the candidate up front it's for honest calibration AND to learn the bar, not a gotcha. A claim that doesn't hold up is unverified and must not inflate the profile or be featured. Set verified_ceiling to the highest level the interview evidence supports — it caps an over-claim and, for a no-portfolio candidate, ESTABLISHES the level. role_fit interviews are repeatable PRACTICE; re-running as the candidate improves is the point. After the interview, re-run assess_competency with the now-demonstrated evidence and turn each delta into an upskilling-plan item.

output_schema.items: [{ dimension?, claim?, question, answer_summary, exemplar, score: weak|adequate|strong, ownership?, understanding?, delta_notes }]
output_schema.verified_ceiling: one of the ladder — the highest level the interview supports (caps/establishes the level)
output_schema.summary: string — the honest read after questioning: what held up, what didn't, the biggest deltas
